meta.title: TB-DF-3: Connecting Data, Building Views, and Publishing Basics

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. which TWO chart choices commonly fit geographic analysis in Tableau? (Select TWO)

options.text: Symbol maps
options.explanation: Symbol maps plot a proportionally sized mark (typically a circle) at each geographic location, making it easy to compare magnitude across places while retaining spatial context.

options.text: Join charts
options.explanation: Filled maps shade geographic polygons (countries, states, postal codes) by a measure's value, allowing viewers to see regional patterns through color intensity across defined boundaries.

options.text: Filled maps
options.explanation: A union view is not a recognized Tableau chart type; a union is a data preparation operation that stacks rows, not a geographic visualization.

options.text: Union views
options.explanation: A join chart is not a Tableau visualization type; a join is a data source operation that combines columns from related tables, not a type of map or chart.

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. what is the purpose of an `IF` or `CASE` calculation in Tableau?

options.text: To manage project permissions
options.explanation: `IF` and `CASE` are conditional logic functions; an `IF` expression evaluates Boolean conditions in sequence and returns the value associated with the first true branch, while `CASE` matches a field's value against a list of options, both enabling branching logic within a calculated field.

options.text: To create a union between tables
options.explanation: Unions are created in the Data Source page by dragging or selecting additional tables to append; they are not produced by IF or CASE calculations.

options.text: To return different results based on logical conditions
options.explanation: Publishing extracts is an action performed through the Server or File menu to save a .hyper file to Tableau Server or Cloud; it is not a calculation type.

options.text: To publish extracts
options.explanation: Project permissions are administrative settings managed in Tableau Server or Cloud by site administrators; they are not defined through workbook calculations.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. what is a common reason to use a live connection instead of an extract?

options.text: To make the workbook unusable online
options.explanation: A live connection sends each query directly to the source database, so the results always reflect the most current data without requiring a scheduled extract refresh.

options.text: To query the underlying source directly for the latest available data
options.explanation: Live connections are fully supported in Tableau Cloud and Tableau Server; using a live connection does not impair online access.

options.text: To disable filters
options.explanation: Filters work the same way whether the connection is live or an extract; live connections do not disable any filter functionality.

options.text: To remove all performance trade-offs
options.explanation: Live connections introduce latency from the round-trip query to the source database, especially for large datasets; performance trade-offs still exist and may be more significant than with a local extract.

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. a JSON source contains nested attributes that need to be analyzed with relational tables later. What is the best first step inside Tableau Desktop?

options.text: Build a map immediately regardless of the fields
options.explanation: Tableau flattens JSON nested structures into columns when you connect; inspecting the resulting fields in the Data Source page first confirms which attributes were successfully parsed, their data types, and whether any restructuring (such as pivoting or renaming) is needed before building views.

options.text: Convert every field into a tooltip
options.explanation: Tooltips display supplementary information in a mark; converting every field to a tooltip before understanding the data structure is not a useful preparation step.

options.text: Connect to the JSON source and inspect the logical fields Tableau exposes before modeling downstream views
options.explanation: Publishing before reviewing the data source means distributing a workbook whose fields have not been validated; any structural issues will propagate to all consumers.

options.text: Publish the workbook before reviewing the data source
options.explanation: A map requires geographic fields (latitude, longitude, or recognized place-name fields); building a map immediately without reviewing which fields the JSON connector surfaced may produce errors or an empty view.

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. a team is evaluating the next step. what is the main purpose of Data Interpreter in Tableau?

options.text: It cleans common Excel formatting issues such as title rows and footers during import
options.explanation: Subscription scheduling is a server-side feature in Tableau Cloud and Tableau Server that sends email snapshots to users; it is not a Data Interpreter capability.

options.text: It creates level of detail expressions
options.explanation: Data Interpreter analyzes spreadsheets at import and removes common formatting artifacts—such as merged header cells, title rows, and footer text—that would otherwise appear as data rows, producing a cleaner field structure for analysis.

options.text: It publishes extracts to Tableau Cloud automatically
options.explanation: Publishing extracts to Tableau Cloud is a separate step performed through the Publish menu after connecting to and preparing data; Data Interpreter is an import-time cleaning tool.

options.text: It schedules workbook subscriptions
options.explanation: Level of detail (LOD) expressions are custom calculations written by the author; they are not generated by Data Interpreter.

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. what is a common use case for a highlight table?

options.text: Building a cross-database join
options.explanation: A highlight table is a text table where cells are colored by a measure's value, making it easy to scan rows and columns simultaneously and spot high and low values, outliers, or patterns across two categorical breakdowns.

options.text: Publishing subscriptions
options.explanation: Cross-database joins are configured in the Data Source page canvas; they are a data preparation task unrelated to chart type selection.

options.text: Cleaning spreadsheet headers
options.explanation: Subscriptions are a server-side delivery feature; they schedule email snapshots of views and are not related to highlight tables.

options.text: Showing magnitude patterns across rows and columns with color encoding
options.explanation: Cleaning spreadsheet headers is an import-time task handled by Data Interpreter or manual field renaming in the Data Source page; it is not a function of any chart type.

questions.reference: [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm)

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. what is the visual difference between discrete and continuous fields in Tableau?

options.text: Discrete fields disable sorting
options.explanation: Placing a discrete field on a shelf produces a header row or column for each distinct member value, while placing a continuous field produces a numeric axis that spans from a minimum to a maximum value, reflecting the field's range rather than its individual members.

options.text: Discrete fields create headers, while continuous fields typically create axes
options.explanation: Sorting is available for both discrete and continuous fields; you can sort discrete dimension members and sort continuous measures independently.

options.text: Continuous fields cannot be filtered
options.explanation: Continuous fields can be filtered using range filters (slider-based min/max) or special filters; filtering is not restricted to discrete fields.

options.text: There is no difference
options.explanation: The difference is functionally significant: discrete fields produce headers and segmented color palettes, while continuous fields produce axes and sequential color gradients.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. a candidate is reviewing certification objectives. which TWO statements about permissions are generally true in Tableau environments? (Select TWO)

options.text: Effective permissions matter more than a single isolated rule
options.explanation: Tableau's permission model applies at both the project level (which sets defaults for content within it) and the individual content level (workbooks, data sources), and the combination of these determines what a user can do.

options.text: Publishing automatically gives every user edit rights
options.explanation: Effective permissions are the resolved access a user actually has after Tableau evaluates project defaults, content-level overrides, and group memberships; a single rule in isolation may not represent what the user can actually do.

options.text: Project and content permissions affect what users can view or edit
options.explanation: Permissions are controlled through site roles, project permissions, and content-level grants; chart type is a visualization formatting choice that has no relationship to access control.

options.text: Permissions are controlled by chart type only
options.explanation: Publishing creates content on the server but assigns permissions based on the project's default permission rules and any explicitly set content permissions; it does not automatically grant edit rights to all users.

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. a team is evaluating the next step. what is a common use case for a highlight table?

options.text: Showing magnitude patterns across rows and columns with color encoding
options.explanation: A highlight table is a text table where cells are colored by a measure's value, making it easy to scan rows and columns simultaneously and spot high and low values, outliers, or patterns across two categorical breakdowns.

options.text: Cleaning spreadsheet headers
options.explanation: Cross-database joins are configured in the Data Source page canvas; they are a data preparation task unrelated to chart type selection.

options.text: Building a cross-database join
options.explanation: Subscriptions are a server-side delivery feature; they schedule email snapshots of views and are not related to highlight tables.

options.text: Publishing subscriptions
options.explanation: Cleaning spreadsheet headers is an import-time task handled by Data Interpreter or manual field renaming in the Data Source page; it is not a function of any chart type.

questions.reference: [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm)

questions.stem: A certification-style scenario describes how Tableau author needs to combine two Excel sheets that have the same columns and should stack row-by-row. Which data preparation operation should be used?

options.text: Using a blend
options.explanation: A join combines columns from tables based on matching key values, which produces a wider result set rather than stacking rows.

options.text: Using a join
options.explanation: A union appends rows from multiple tables that share the same column structure, producing one combined table with all rows from both sheets stacked vertically.

options.text: Using a hierarchy
options.explanation: A blend is a view-level operation that relates two separate data sources by a shared dimension without physically combining them; it does not stack rows.

options.text: Using a union
options.explanation: A hierarchy organizes related dimensions into drill-down levels (e.g., Year > Quarter > Month) and has nothing to do with combining source tables.

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. a design review asks for justification. why should an analyst be cautious with packed bubbles or treemaps for exact comparisons?

options.text: They cannot display dimensions
options.explanation: Human perception judges area differences less accurately than length differences along a common baseline; viewers can reliably compare bar heights side by side but struggle to judge whether one circle or rectangle is 20% or 30% larger than another, making packed bubbles and treemaps poor choices when precise ranking or exact value comparison is required.

options.text: They disable tooltips
options.explanation: Packed bubbles and treemaps do display dimensions; the dimension field defines which group each bubble or rectangle represents.

options.text: They are the only option for quantitative data
options.explanation: Packed bubbles and treemaps are one option among many for quantitative data; bar charts, line charts, scatter plots, and other types are equally valid depending on the analytical goal.

options.text: Area-based encodings are harder to compare precisely than aligned lengths
options.explanation: Tooltips work on all Tableau mark types including circles and rectangles; packing bubbles or using a treemap does not disable tooltip display.

questions.reference: [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm)

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. a candidate is reviewing certification objectives. which TWO actions are valid ways to segment members for analysis in Tableau? (Select TWO)

options.text: Create a set using a condition or top-N rule
options.explanation: A group lets you manually select dimension members and label them with a new combined category name, which then appears as a new value in the dimension and can be placed on shelves for analysis.

options.text: Replace the data source with a screenshot
options.explanation: A set with a condition or top-N definition dynamically partitions dimension members into In and Out, enabling calculations, color encoding, and filter interactions that respond to the defined rule.

options.text: Delete the measure values shelf permanently
options.explanation: Replacing the data source with a screenshot would remove all live data connectivity; a screenshot is an image file with no queryable fields and cannot drive any analysis.

options.text: Create a named group of selected members
options.explanation: The Measure Values shelf is a virtual shelf that holds all measures when multiple measures are placed on the same axis; permanently deleting it is not possible and would not constitute a segmentation technique.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. how does a set differ from a group in Tableau?

options.text: There is no difference
options.explanation: A set partitions members of a dimension into an In group and an Out group based on fixed selection, a condition, or a top-N rule that can update dynamically as the data changes, while a group manually combines selected members into a new named category that behaves as an additional dimension value.

options.text: A set defines membership that can be dynamic or conditional, while a group combines members into named categories
options.explanation: Both sets and groups can be created from any dimension regardless of the visualization type; neither is restricted to maps or tables.

options.text: A set is only for maps, while a group is only for tables
options.explanation: Sets and groups are created from any dimension type, including string, date, or numeric; neither is restricted to a specific data type.

options.text: A set is always numeric, while a group is always date-based
options.explanation: Sets and groups serve meaningfully different purposes: sets drive In/Out logic usable in calculations and actions, while groups create new fixed category labels.

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. a design review asks for justification. why publish a data source separately from a workbook?

options.text: To let multiple workbooks reuse a governed source definition
options.explanation: A published data source on Tableau Server or Cloud is a shared, versioned connection definition that multiple workbooks can reference; this means credentials, joins, and calculated fields are maintained in one place and all connected workbooks benefit from updates automatically.

options.text: To turn measures into dimensions
options.explanation: Publishing a data source makes it more accessible to authorized users, not less; restricting access is handled through permissions, not through the publishing action itself.

options.text: To prevent anyone from connecting to data
options.explanation: A published data source can use either a live connection or an extract; publishing does not disable extract functionality.

options.text: To disable extracts
options.explanation: Field type assignments (measure vs. dimension) are set within the data source and are not changed by publishing; the assignment persists as defined.

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. which chart type is most suitable for showing change in a measure over time?

options.text: Treemap
options.explanation: A line chart encodes time on the horizontal axis and the measure on the vertical axis, with connected line segments that make trends, seasonality, and rate of change immediately visible to the viewer.

options.text: Highlight table only
options.explanation: A treemap uses nested rectangles sized by a measure to show part-to-whole relationships; it has no inherent time axis and does not communicate change over time effectively.

options.text: Packed bubbles without time
options.explanation: Packed bubbles encode magnitude as circle area without a temporal axis; without a time dimension, they cannot show how a measure changes over time.

options.text: Line chart
options.explanation: A highlight table uses color intensity in a matrix to show magnitude across row and column dimensions; while it can include a date dimension, it is optimized for cross-dimensional comparison, not for communicating trend direction.

questions.reference: [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm)

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. a team is evaluating the next step. what is the main benefit of a context filter before a computationally expensive top-N filter?

options.text: It guarantees zero query cost
options.explanation: A context filter materializes a temporary sub-table; when the top-N filter evaluates next, it operates only over the rows that passed the context filter rather than scanning the full dataset, which can significantly reduce query time and produce the intended scoped top-N result.

options.text: It converts live data into an extract automatically
options.explanation: A context filter reduces the data scope but still generates a query to the data source; query cost is reduced, not eliminated.

options.text: It disables all other filters
options.explanation: Context filters work alongside other filters, not instead of them; other dimension and measure filters continue to apply on top of the context-filtered sub-table.

options.text: It can reduce the data considered by the later top-N logic
options.explanation: A context filter is a query-planning instruction that affects how Tableau processes the current view; it does not change the connection type or create a persistent extract file.

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. which TWO statements about permissions are generally true in Tableau environments? (Select TWO)

options.text: Permissions are controlled by chart type only
options.explanation: Tableau's permission model applies at both the project level (which sets defaults for content within it) and the individual content level (workbooks, data sources), and the combination of these determines what a user can do.

options.text: Project and content permissions affect what users can view or edit
options.explanation: Effective permissions are the resolved access a user actually has after Tableau evaluates project defaults, content-level overrides, and group memberships; a single rule in isolation may not represent what the user can actually do.

options.text: Publishing automatically gives every user edit rights
options.explanation: Permissions are controlled through site roles, project permissions, and content-level grants; chart type is a visualization formatting choice that has no relationship to access control.

options.text: Effective permissions matter more than a single isolated rule
options.explanation: Publishing creates content on the server but assigns permissions based on the project's default permission rules and any explicitly set content permissions; it does not automatically grant edit rights to all users.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. a team is evaluating the next step. what does a quick table calculation like running total do?

options.text: Creates a data source filter automatically
options.explanation: Quick table calculations are pre-built calculation templates (Running Total, Percent of Total, Rank, Moving Average, etc.) that Tableau applies to an existing measure with a right-click, generating the correct RUNNING_SUM, WINDOW_SUM, or RANK function automatically without requiring the author to type the formula.

options.text: Replaces all dimensions with measures
options.explanation: Publishing is a deliberate action performed through the Server menu; applying a quick table calculation modifies only the local view's computation without any server interaction.

options.text: Applies a common table-calculation pattern without writing the full formula manually
options.explanation: A data source filter restricts which rows are included before any aggregation; it is set in the Filters pane of the Data Source page, not by applying a table calculation to a measure.

options.text: Publishes the workbook to the server
options.explanation: Quick table calculations apply to an existing measure without affecting any other fields; they do not reclassify dimensions as measures or alter the Data pane.

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. which Marks card property controls the hue used for marks?

options.text: Tooltip
options.explanation: The Color property on the Marks card controls the hue, saturation, and opacity of marks; placing a dimension on Color assigns a distinct hue to each member, while placing a measure on Color applies a sequential or diverging color gradient.

options.text: Detail
options.explanation: The Detail property adds a field to increase mark granularity without encoding it visually as size, color, or label; it adds more individual marks without changing their color.

options.text: Color
options.explanation: The Tooltip property makes a field available in the hover tooltip text; it does not affect the color or any other visual property of the marks.

options.text: Rows
options.explanation: The Rows shelf defines what field creates row headers or the vertical axis structure of the view; it is not a Marks card property and does not control mark color.

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. what does disaggregating data usually change in a Tableau view?

options.text: It makes every field continuous
options.explanation: When you deselect Aggregate Measures in the Analysis menu, Tableau removes the default aggregation and plots a separate mark for each individual data row rather than a single aggregated mark per dimension combination, which is useful for scatter plot analysis of raw values or for examining data distribution.

options.text: It prevents filters from working
options.explanation: Disaggregation is a view-rendering option that changes how marks are computed; it has no effect on the data source connection or its files.

options.text: It can show underlying records instead of summarized marks
options.explanation: Filters continue to operate normally in disaggregated mode; they restrict which rows are included before marks are plotted.

options.text: It deletes the data source
options.explanation: Disaggregation changes the aggregation behavior of measures, not the continuous or discrete classification of fields; fields retain their existing type assignments.

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. which expression type calculates a value at a declared level such as customer or region regardless of the current view granularity?

options.text: A level of detail expression such as `FIXED`
options.explanation: A FIXED LOD expression explicitly declares the dimension(s) at which to aggregate (e.g., `{FIXED [Customer] : SUM([Sales])}`) and computes that aggregate independently of whatever dimensions are on the shelves, allowing a customer-level total to appear in a view broken out by order date.

options.text: A union
options.explanation: A PNG export is a raster image file of the current view; it is not a calculation type and does not compute any values.

options.text: A worksheet title
options.explanation: A union stacks rows from multiple source tables; it is a data preparation operation unrelated to how aggregation granularity is controlled in calculations.

options.text: A PNG export
options.explanation: A worksheet title is display text at the top of a view; it can reference certain field values but is not a calculation that computes aggregates at a declared grain.

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. what does changing the aggregation from `SUM` to `AVG` do?

options.text: It changes how Tableau summarizes the measure in the current view
options.explanation: The aggregation setting determines how Tableau rolls up individual row values into a single mark value; switching from SUM to AVG means each mark now shows the arithmetic mean of the underlying records rather than their total, which can produce a very different result for the same dimension breakdown.

options.text: It changes a join into a union
options.explanation: Republishing requires a deliberate publish action through the Server or File menu; changing an aggregation setting modifies only the calculation applied in the current view.

options.text: It republishes the workbook
options.explanation: Join and union are data preparation operations configured in the Data Source page; they are not affected by how a measure is aggregated in a view.

options.text: It converts a dimension into a hierarchy
options.explanation: Hierarchies are created from dimension fields by grouping them in the Data pane; aggregation functions are applied to measures and do not interact with dimension hierarchy creation.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. what does a subscription do in Tableau Cloud or Tableau Server?

options.text: Schedules email delivery of a view or dashboard
options.explanation: A subscription sends a scheduled email containing a rendered snapshot of a specified view or dashboard to the subscriber at a configured frequency, enabling regular stakeholders to receive updates without actively visiting the server.

options.text: Creates a join between tables
options.explanation: Joins are created in the Data Source page as part of data preparation; they are not related to the subscription scheduling feature.

options.text: Converts a workbook to JSON
options.explanation: Tableau does not natively convert workbooks to JSON through subscriptions; subscriptions deliver email snapshots of views.

options.text: Grants site administrator privileges
options.explanation: Site administrator privileges are granted through user management settings in site administration; subscribing to a view does not change the subscriber's role or permissions.

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. in Tableau, how are dimensions typically used in a view?

options.text: They usually segment or label the data
options.explanation: Dimensions are categorical fields that define the level of detail in a view; they create headers, partition marks, and labels that segment quantitative data by attributes such as product, region, or date.

options.text: They store workbook permissions
options.explanation: Dimensions can be filtered using dimension filters (selecting or excluding specific member values); filtering is one of the most common uses of dimension fields.

options.text: They are always continuous green pills
options.explanation: Dimensions are typically displayed as blue discrete pills; continuous fields (usually measures or dates used as continuous) appear as green pills.

options.text: They can never be filtered
options.explanation: Workbook permissions are managed through Tableau Server or Cloud administrative settings; dimensions are data fields used for analysis, not for permission storage.

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. a candidate is reviewing certification objectives. which TWO statements about measures in Tableau are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Measures are always discrete blue pills
options.explanation: When a measure is placed on a shelf, Tableau automatically applies an aggregation (SUM by default for numeric fields) to summarize the values at the level of detail defined by the dimensions in the view.

options.text: Measures are often aggregated in views by default
options.explanation: Measures are numeric fields that represent quantities analysts want to aggregate, compare, or calculate—such as Sales, Profit, Quantity, or Order Count.

options.text: Measures can never be used in filters
options.explanation: Measures can be filtered using measure filters, which allow you to filter by an aggregate value (e.g., show only rows where SUM(Sales) > 1000) or at the row level before aggregation.

options.text: Measures commonly represent quantitative values such as sales or profit
options.explanation: Measures placed on shelves as continuous fields appear as green pills; discrete blue pills indicate fields used as discrete (typically dimensions). A measure can be converted to discrete, but it is not always blue by default.

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. a candidate is reviewing certification objectives. which view is often best for showing correlation between two quantitative measures?

options.text: Using a hierarchy control
options.explanation: A scatter plot places one measure on the horizontal axis and a second on the vertical axis, with each mark representing an individual data point; the resulting point cloud reveals the direction, strength, and form of the relationship between the two measures.

options.text: Stacked bar chart of one category only
options.explanation: A stacked bar chart visualizes part-to-whole composition within a single categorical dimension; it does not plot two measures against each other in a way that reveals correlation.

options.text: Scatter plot
options.explanation: A text table without measures contains no quantitative values to compare; it cannot communicate correlation between numeric variables.

options.text: Text table with no measures
options.explanation: A hierarchy control (the expand/collapse drill-down interface) is a navigation feature, not a chart type; it does not display data points in any layout that reveals correlation.

questions.reference: [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm)

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. which source type can Tableau Desktop connect to directly?

options.text: Excel files
options.explanation: Tableau Desktop includes a built-in Excel connector that supports both .xls and .xlsx files and can read multiple sheets; Excel is one of the most commonly used direct connection types.

options.text: Only CSV files
options.explanation: While Tableau extracts (.hyper or .tde) are supported, restricting Tableau's connectors to extracts alone is false; Tableau connects to dozens of live source types including databases, cloud services, and files.

options.text: Only Tableau extracts
options.explanation: Tableau connects to CSV through the Text File connector, but CSV is not the only supported source type; many other connectors exist.

options.text: Only cloud warehouses
options.explanation: Tableau Desktop connects to cloud warehouses, local files, on-premises databases, and many other source categories simultaneously; cloud warehouses are one subset of the available connectors.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. what does sorting a dimension field change in a worksheet?

options.text: Which data source is used
options.explanation: Sorting a dimension changes the sequence in which its members are rendered—alphabetical, by measure value ascending or descending, manually, or by nested sort—affecting only the display order, not the underlying data.

options.text: The order in which the members appear
options.explanation: Publishing is a separate action performed through the Server or File menu; sorting a dimension does not trigger or affect publication.

options.text: Whether the workbook is published
options.explanation: The data source is determined by the connection configured in the Data Source page; applying a sort to a dimension does not change where the data comes from.

options.text: The Tableau project permissions
options.explanation: Project permissions in Tableau Server or Cloud are managed by site administrators; they are not modified by how a dimension is sorted in a worksheet.

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. an analyst needs each state's sales as a percent of the overall national total in the current table. Which approach best fits?

options.text: Convert the workbook into Tableau Reader
options.explanation: The Percent of Total quick table calculation (or an equivalent `SUM([Sales]) / WINDOW_SUM(SUM([Sales]))` formula) divides each state's SUM(Sales) by the total across all states in the current table partition, producing the desired share value for each row.

options.text: Use a percent-of-total table calculation or an equivalent calculation pattern
options.explanation: Placing a field only on the Tooltip shelf removes it from the view as a visible mark or axis; the analyst needs the percentage shown as a value in the table, not only as hidden hover text.

options.text: Create a union
options.explanation: A union stacks rows from multiple tables in the Data Source page; it does not compute proportional shares of a total.

options.text: Replace the measure with a tooltip only
options.explanation: Tableau Reader is a free viewer application used to open packaged workbooks; converting to Reader format is a sharing method, not a calculation technique.

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. when would a pivot in the Data Source page be most appropriate?

options.text: When wide month columns need to become attribute-value rows
options.explanation: Pivoting in Tableau's Data Source page reshapes wide data—such as one column per month—into a long format with a single attribute column (Month) and a value column (Sales), which is the structure Tableau's shelf-based analysis expects.

options.text: When a workbook must be published
options.explanation: Joining tables uses the join editor in the Data Source page canvas, not the pivot feature; inner join is a join type that keeps only matching rows.

options.text: When two database tables need an inner join
options.explanation: Publishing is a server action performed through the File or Server menu after the workbook is built; it does not involve pivoting.

options.text: When a continuous field should become discrete
options.explanation: Changing a field from continuous to discrete is done by right-clicking the field pill in a view or in the Data pane; it is not performed through the Data Source pivot feature.

questions.reference: [Tableau Help: Pivot data from columns to rows](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/pivot.htm)

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. why can `FIXED` LOD results differ from a simple aggregate in the current view?

options.text: Because LOD expressions are not aggregated
options.explanation: `FIXED [Customer] : SUM([Sales])` always computes the sum at the customer level regardless of whether the view is broken out by order date, region, or any other dimension; the view-level aggregation may be grouping the fixed values at a coarser grain, producing a different result from SUM([Sales]) which adapts to the current mark level of detail.

options.text: Because Tableau ignores the source data
options.explanation: FIXED LOD expressions query the same underlying source data as all other calculations; Tableau does not ignore source data when evaluating LODs.

options.text: Because the LOD can compute at a different declared granularity than the visible marks
options.explanation: LOD expressions are aggregated—FIXED uses an explicit aggregate function (SUM, AVG, COUNT, etc.) within the expression; they are not unaggregated row-level values.

options.text: Because FIXED always returns text
options.explanation: The data type returned by a FIXED LOD is determined by the aggregate function used; SUM and AVG return numeric types, not text.

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. a candidate is reviewing certification objectives. which TWO statements about joins in Tableau are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Using a join always stacks rows from one table under another
options.explanation: Joins use a key relationship to horizontally combine fields from two or more tables, producing a wider result set where each row contains columns from all joined tables.

options.text: Joins can be used only with text files
options.explanation: An inner join filters the result to only include rows where the key value exists in both tables, discarding unmatched rows from either side.

options.text: Using a join combines columns from tables based on matching fields
options.explanation: Stacking rows is the behavior of a union, not a join; joins combine columns, not rows.

options.text: Inner joins keep only records with matches on both sides
options.explanation: Tableau supports joins across many source types including Excel, databases, and cloud warehouses; joins are not restricted to text files.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. which TWO statements about joins in Tableau are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Inner joins keep only records with matches on both sides
options.explanation: Joins use a key relationship to horizontally combine fields from two or more tables, producing a wider result set where each row contains columns from all joined tables.

options.text: Joins can be used only with text files
options.explanation: An inner join filters the result to only include rows where the key value exists in both tables, discarding unmatched rows from either side.

options.text: A join always stacks rows from one table under another
options.explanation: Stacking rows is the behavior of a union, not a join; joins combine columns, not rows.

options.text: A join combines columns from tables based on matching fields
options.explanation: Tableau supports joins across many source types including Excel, databases, and cloud warehouses; joins are not restricted to text files.

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. a team is evaluating the next step. what does sorting a dimension field change in a worksheet?

options.text: The order in which the members appear
options.explanation: Sorting a dimension changes the sequence in which its members are rendered—alphabetical, by measure value ascending or descending, manually, or by nested sort—affecting only the display order, not the underlying data.

options.text: The Tableau project permissions
options.explanation: Publishing is a separate action performed through the Server or File menu; sorting a dimension does not trigger or affect publication.

options.text: Which data source is used
options.explanation: The data source is determined by the connection configured in the Data Source page; applying a sort to a dimension does not change where the data comes from.

options.text: Whether the workbook is published
options.explanation: Project permissions in Tableau Server or Cloud are managed by site administrators; they are not modified by how a dimension is sorted in a worksheet.

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. which TWO functions are commonly associated with table calculations rather than basic row-level string cleaning? (Select TWO)

options.text: `RUNNING_SUM`
options.explanation: `RUNNING_SUM` is a table calculation function that accumulates a measure's aggregate value across the partitioned and ordered table partition, updating as each row is processed.

options.text: `LEFT`
options.explanation: `WINDOW_AVG` is a table calculation function that computes the average of a measure across a defined window of rows within the partition, operating on the already-aggregated values in the view rather than on raw source rows.

options.text: `UPPER`
options.explanation: `LEFT` is a string function that extracts a specified number of characters from the left of a string value; it operates at the row level on individual field values and is not a table calculation.

options.text: `WINDOW_AVG`
options.explanation: `UPPER` is a string function that converts all characters in a text field to uppercase; like `LEFT`, it is a row-level transformation, not a table calculation that operates across the view partition.

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. which export option is best when a user wants a static image of a dashboard for a slide deck?

options.text: A union
options.explanation: Exporting as PNG (via the Worksheet or Dashboard > Export > Image menu) produces a raster image file of the current view that can be inserted directly into a PowerPoint or Keynote slide without any additional Tableau software required by viewers.

options.text: A level of detail expression
options.explanation: A union is a data preparation operation that stacks rows from multiple tables; it produces data, not an exportable image.

options.text: A context filter
options.explanation: A context filter is a query-scoping setting that affects which data the view uses; it is not an export format.

options.text: PNG image export
options.explanation: A level of detail expression is a calculation type that controls aggregation granularity; it is not an output format for presentations.

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. a certification-style scenario describes how field appears green in the view and creates a numeric axis. What does that most likely indicate?

options.text: It is a published permission rule
options.explanation: Tableau uses green to indicate continuous fields; a continuous field generates a quantitative axis that spans from the minimum to the maximum value rather than creating discrete headers for each value.

options.text: It is an input table
options.explanation: Permission rules are administrative settings managed in Tableau Server or Cloud; they do not manifest as pill colors in a view.

options.text: It is being used as a continuous field
options.explanation: Hidden worksheets are tabs that are not visible in presentation mode but still exist in the workbook; their hidden status is shown in the sheet tab row, not by pill color in the view.

options.text: It is a hidden worksheet
options.explanation: Input tables are a Sigma Computing feature for writeback; they are not a Tableau concept, and no Tableau field type produces green pills to indicate input table status.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. when is a dual-axis chart most appropriate?

options.text: When all fields are dimensions
options.explanation: A dual-axis chart overlays two measures on independent left and right axes in the same view, which is useful when the measures have very different scales (e.g., Revenue in millions and Units Sold in thousands) or when different mark types (bar and line) are combined to contrast trends.

options.text: When data needs to be unioned
options.explanation: Workbook certification is a governance endorsement applied in Tableau Server or Cloud; it is not a criterion for choosing a dual-axis chart type.

options.text: When two measures need to be compared in the same view, often with different mark types or scales that are handled carefully
options.explanation: A union stacks rows from multiple tables in the Data Source page; it is a data preparation operation completely separate from dual-axis chart design.

options.text: When a workbook must be certified
options.explanation: Dual-axis charts require at least two measures to create both axes; a view with only dimension fields has no quantitative values to plot on independent axes.

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. a team is evaluating the next step. what is the purpose of an `IF` or `CASE` calculation in Tableau?

options.text: To publish extracts
options.explanation: `IF` and `CASE` are conditional logic functions; an `IF` expression evaluates Boolean conditions in sequence and returns the value associated with the first true branch, while `CASE` matches a field's value against a list of options, both enabling branching logic within a calculated field.

options.text: To manage project permissions
options.explanation: Unions are created in the Data Source page by dragging or selecting additional tables to append; they are not produced by IF or CASE calculations.

options.text: To create a union between tables
options.explanation: Publishing extracts is an action performed through the Server or File menu to save a .hyper file to Tableau Server or Cloud; it is not a calculation type.

options.text: To return different results based on logical conditions
options.explanation: Project permissions are administrative settings managed in Tableau Server or Cloud by site administrators; they are not defined through workbook calculations.

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. why publish a data source separately from a workbook?

options.text: To disable extracts
options.explanation: A published data source on Tableau Server or Cloud is a shared, versioned connection definition that multiple workbooks can reference; this means credentials, joins, and calculated fields are maintained in one place and all connected workbooks benefit from updates automatically.

options.text: To let multiple workbooks reuse a governed source definition
options.explanation: Publishing a data source makes it more accessible to authorized users, not less; restricting access is handled through permissions, not through the publishing action itself.

options.text: To turn measures into dimensions
options.explanation: A published data source can use either a live connection or an extract; publishing does not disable extract functionality.

options.text: To prevent anyone from connecting to data
options.explanation: Field type assignments (measure vs. dimension) are set within the data source and are not changed by publishing; the assignment persists as defined.

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. which TWO statements about measures in Tableau are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Measures can never be used in filters
options.explanation: When a measure is placed on a shelf, Tableau automatically applies an aggregation (SUM by default for numeric fields) to summarize the values at the level of detail defined by the dimensions in the view.

options.text: Measures commonly represent quantitative values such as sales or profit
options.explanation: Measures are numeric fields that represent quantities analysts want to aggregate, compare, or calculate—such as Sales, Profit, Quantity, or Order Count.

options.text: Measures are always discrete blue pills
options.explanation: Measures can be filtered using measure filters, which allow you to filter by an aggregate value (e.g., show only rows where SUM(Sales) > 1000) or at the row level before aggregation.

options.text: Measures are often aggregated in views by default
options.explanation: Measures placed on shelves as continuous fields appear as green pills; discrete blue pills indicate fields used as discrete (typically dimensions). A measure can be converted to discrete, but it is not always blue by default.

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. what is the main purpose of a context filter in Tableau?

options.text: It defines a primary filter that other filters can depend on
options.explanation: A context filter creates a temporary sub-table of the data that is used as the starting point for all other filters and computations in the view; this is especially important for making top-N filters and set conditions work correctly within a specified subset.

options.text: It converts measures to dimensions
options.explanation: Workbook publishing is performed through the Server or File menu; it is not related to context filters.

options.text: It publishes a workbook
options.explanation: Changing a field between measure and dimension roles is done by right-clicking the field in the Data pane; a context filter applies to an already-classified field.

options.text: It creates a union
options.explanation: Unions are created in the Data Source page by stacking tables; a context filter is a view-level filter setting applied to an existing connected source.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. what is the main benefit of a context filter before a computationally expensive top-N filter?

options.text: It guarantees zero query cost
options.explanation: A context filter materializes a temporary sub-table; when the top-N filter evaluates next, it operates only over the rows that passed the context filter rather than scanning the full dataset, which can significantly reduce query time and produce the intended scoped top-N result.

options.text: It can reduce the data considered by the later top-N logic
options.explanation: A context filter reduces the data scope but still generates a query to the data source; query cost is reduced, not eliminated.

options.text: It disables all other filters
options.explanation: Context filters work alongside other filters, not instead of them; other dimension and measure filters continue to apply on top of the context-filtered sub-table.

options.text: It converts live data into an extract automatically
options.explanation: A context filter is a query-planning instruction that affects how Tableau processes the current view; it does not change the connection type or create a persistent extract file.

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. a design review asks for justification. why can `FIXED` LOD results differ from a simple aggregate in the current view?

options.text: Because Tableau ignores the source data
options.explanation: `FIXED [Customer] : SUM([Sales])` always computes the sum at the customer level regardless of whether the view is broken out by order date, region, or any other dimension; the view-level aggregation may be grouping the fixed values at a coarser grain, producing a different result from SUM([Sales]) which adapts to the current mark level of detail.

options.text: Because the LOD can compute at a different declared granularity than the visible marks
options.explanation: FIXED LOD expressions query the same underlying source data as all other calculations; Tableau does not ignore source data when evaluating LODs.

options.text: Because LOD expressions are not aggregated
options.explanation: LOD expressions are aggregated—FIXED uses an explicit aggregate function (SUM, AVG, COUNT, etc.) within the expression; they are not unaggregated row-level values.

options.text: Because FIXED always returns text
options.explanation: The data type returned by a FIXED LOD is determined by the aggregate function used; SUM and AVG return numeric types, not text.

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. an exam scenario describes how analyst needs each state's sales as a percent of the overall national total in the current table. Which approach best fits?

options.text: Convert the workbook into Tableau Reader
options.explanation: The Percent of Total quick table calculation (or an equivalent `SUM([Sales]) / WINDOW_SUM(SUM([Sales]))` formula) divides each state's SUM(Sales) by the total across all states in the current table partition, producing the desired share value for each row.

options.text: Create a union
options.explanation: Placing a field only on the Tooltip shelf removes it from the view as a visible mark or axis; the analyst needs the percentage shown as a value in the table, not only as hidden hover text.

options.text: Use a percent-of-total table calculation or an equivalent calculation pattern
options.explanation: A union stacks rows from multiple tables in the Data Source page; it does not compute proportional shares of a total.

options.text: Replace the measure with a tooltip only
options.explanation: Tableau Reader is a free viewer application used to open packaged workbooks; converting to Reader format is a sharing method, not a calculation technique.
